GDL-01.title: Medical Consultation Required
GDL-01.snippet: Antibiotics should only be taken when prescribed by a certified health professional. Using antibiotics without medical guidance increases the risk of inappropriate use and contributes to antimicrobial resistance.

GDL-02.title: Complete the Prescribed Course
GDL-02.snippet: Stopping an antibiotic course before completion leaves partially resistant bacteria alive. These survivors multiply, passing on genetic mutations that make future infections harder to treat.

GDL-03.title: Avoid Self-Medication
GDL-03.snippet: Self-medicating often means using the wrong drug or dosage. This creates intense selective pressure, killing weak bacteria but allowing mutated, drug-resistant strains to thrive and spread.

GDL-04.title: Antibiotics vs Viral Infections
GDL-04.snippet: Antibiotics do not kill viruses. Using them for viral illnesses offers no cure, but unnecessarily exposes your body's healthy microbiome to evolutionary pressure, selecting for resistant 'superbugs'.

GDL-05.title: Dose Adherence Matters
GDL-05.snippet: Skipping doses creates sub-therapeutic drug levels in the bloodstream. This gives bacteria time to rapidly adapt, mutate, and develop biological defenses (like efflux pumps) against the antibiotic.

GDL-06.title: Frequent Antibiotic Use
GDL-06.snippet: Repeated antibiotic use applies constant selective pressure on your microbiome. Bacteria are forced to evolve defenses or share resistance genes (plasmids) with other bacteria to survive.

GDL-07.title: Never Share Antibiotics
GDL-07.snippet: Sharing antibiotics with family members or friends means the wrong drug for the wrong infection. This dangerous practice promotes the spread of resistant bacteria.
